claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my son Alex, who is 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'd like to ask some questions about your son Alex to determine eligibility.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he attending school, college, sixth form, or an approved training program?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he's not in college or sixth form anymore. He's actually started an apprenticeship recently.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is important for determining eligibility.

**Can you tell me what type of apprenticeship Alex has started? Specifically, is it an apprenticeship in England?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's an apprenticeship in England. He's training to be an electrician.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, I need to inform you of the outcome:

According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. Since Alex has started an apprenticeship in England as an electrician, this means you would no longer be eligible for Child Benefit for him.

Let me record this assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry, but based on your circumstances, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**. 

The reason is that Alex has started an apprenticeship in England. Under Child Benefit rules, payments stop when a child starts an apprenticeship in England, even if they are under 20 years old.

If you have been receiving Child Benefit for Alex, you should contact HMRC to inform them of this change in circumstances so that payments can be stopped and you won't have any overpayments to deal with later.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. Although Alex is under 20, the apprenticeship disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility, regardless of whether he was previously in approved education or training.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who has recently started an apprenticeship in England as an electrician. Under UK Child Benefit rules, a child starting an apprenticeship in England is a specific circumstance that ends Child Benefit entitlement, even though the child is under 20 years old. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. Although Alex is under 20, the apprenticeship disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility, regardless of whether he was previously in approved education or training.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who has recently started an apprenticeship in England as an electrician. Under UK Child Benefit rules, a child starting an apprenticeship in England is a specific circumstance that ends Child Benefit entitlement, even though the child is under 20 years old. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
